​@theoneed2051  maybe he doesn't understand what it is? i think its common for people who don't understand the nature of the mind to misunderstand such things. 
I'm curious to know what you're getting at however, but in my opinion, the term "AI psychosis" is kind of a misnomer anyways. People undergoing psychosis are experiencing a psycho-physiological event. The fact that their interaction with AI was prominent during that process is coincidental. It is often the case that people experiencing psychosis seek out information that facilitates that experience. 
When I experienced psychosis, it was a combination of drugs and the information I sought on the internet, I was searching for a better understanding of reality, and where I fit into it. That is what psychosis is...a seeking for a new framework when the one you had has been broken.

comments.text_display:
@seeknndestroy420 Test your app. Even if it seem to work, test the edge cases. Also, you can ask the AI to help you code better. For example, I never bothered to learn depedency injection (DI) in my WPF app... well, I asked Claude. He analyzed my code. And guess what? In my case, there is almost no benefit. But he advised me to do something else... which I did. Also, when you ask questions on architecture or other things, try to analyze the benefits and cons. I'd say half of the time, Claude is right. The other half... well... he is not in your head. He doesn't know what YOU want to do, because he is not a god. He is a helper. So his assumptions are, a lot of times, wrong! Like the fact that... 90% of the time, the customer doesn't know what he wants, you have to guide him to what he wants!

That being said, the best way to make the AI do great things and not destroy everything is be as precise as you can be with your intent and requirements. NEVER be vague. NEVER! You'll waste a shit-ton of money because he'll go in circles and build shit. Always be precise. Tell them precisely what you want.

If you can, tell how you want it done. I have a ton of parts and components in my app, a lot of time I say "Blablabla in TablePart" or something. Also, look at the code... for example, I started a React app. Claude dumped all of the stuff in one component file... it was a giant, stupid, tsx file, I told him : yeah... it's ugly! Put A, B, and C in components, create a page for the main content and another page for blablabla, etc.

Claude is amazing. I wonder if we'll do something like that, but in another domain.

Also, you want to understand the code, because if not you'll be in a world of pain when things break in prod! So, each time Claude does something, make him structure the code properly. I even do refactoring with Claude, it's amazing. I do stuff I never dared to do because, well... cleaning up and housekeeping code takes time!

BTW, I never generated unit tests with AI. I will do one day. I heard they generate tests that "pass". I wonder if it's true... might be the case... anyway... I test as if I was an end-user...
comments.text_original:
@seeknndestroy420 Test your app. Even if it seem to work, test the edge cases. Also, you can ask the AI to help you code better. For example, I never bothered to learn depedency injection (DI) in my WPF app... well, I asked Claude. He analyzed my code. And guess what? In my case, there is almost no benefit. But he advised me to do something else... which I did. Also, when you ask questions on architecture or other things, try to analyze the benefits and cons. I'd say half of the time, Claude is right. The other half... well... he is not in your head. He doesn't know what YOU want to do, because he is not a god. He is a helper. So his assumptions are, a lot of times, wrong! Like the fact that... 90% of the time, the customer doesn't know what he wants, you have to guide him to what he wants!

That being said, the best way to make the AI do great things and not destroy everything is be as precise as you can be with your intent and requirements. NEVER be vague. NEVER! You'll waste a shit-ton of money because he'll go in circles and build shit. Always be precise. Tell them precisely what you want.

If you can, tell how you want it done. I have a ton of parts and components in my app, a lot of time I say "Blablabla in TablePart" or something. Also, look at the code... for example, I started a React app. Claude dumped all of the stuff in one component file... it was a giant, stupid, tsx file, I told him : yeah... it's ugly! Put A, B, and C in components, create a page for the main content and another page for blablabla, etc.

Claude is amazing. I wonder if we'll do something like that, but in another domain.

Also, you want to understand the code, because if not you'll be in a world of pain when things break in prod! So, each time Claude does something, make him structure the code properly. I even do refactoring with Claude, it's amazing. I do stuff I never dared to do because, well... cleaning up and housekeeping code takes time!

BTW, I never generated unit tests with AI. I will do one day. I heard they generate tests that "pass". I wonder if it's true... might be the case... anyway... I test as if I was an end-user...

I'm quite impressed that Andrew got to the point of 90% to 100% automation in software development using AI. 

So far it's quite hard for me to get AI to deliver a moderately complex feature that uses kind of unique concept and is integrated with existing codebase. Even though the PRD and related files were crafted with care. Maybe that is wrong model, wrong skilss or something else. AI agent is briliant and saves time but always stays short of actually delivering the thing and the produced code needs significant amount of manual work to reach the finish line. 

I'd love to see an example of an 100% AI written software product that works at least as good as analogous product written by humans, established on the market. I do not mean a website or "to do list" app.
Something like enterprise CRM or another application with simillar complexity. 

We know that Antropic used AI to create a browser that did not compile, is almost working, based on existing open source html/css rendering library and spend tokens worth approx 7-14 mln US dollars to do this. It also created linux kernel that does not work very well. So yep those are amazing achivements, but still they do not live up to the promise.

For home automation, home assistant has been around for years and integrates almost anything. It can do everything he has described so there is nothing new. It also auto discovers everything as well. You could call HA an AI in what it does. AI is nothign more than a trained set of rules. If you see this, then this.

In regards to software development, those code assistants can generate large amounts of compileable code of unknown structure or design. It maybe right or maybe wrong.  It might be over engineered or under. You do not know. When you deliver the software to your customer, you do not know how it works. You only see the result.  If the customer finds a problem, you do not know how to fix it so you will go back to AI and ask it to fix the code. Chances are it will rewrite some of the code and create new problems. AI code is very hard to maintain. As I say, would you fly in a plane if they told you an AI agent wrote the flight control system ?

comments.text_display:
About speciation: It bewilders me how or why we don't already have small models for specific things. Or areas. A home robot doesn't need to know algebra. A factory robot needs to know even less. A claw bot doesn't need to know the ancient history of Machu Picchu. Humans don't. A math professor doesn't nee to know crap about biology. A psycologist doesn't know anything about engineering. -- I would have expected this bifurcation to have happened like two years ago.

The less a bot knows, the smaller, faster, and efficient it can be. Everything else is just baggage. A 2-thread 2GB laptop should contain both the claw bot and the AI it needs for thinking. comments.text_display:
I have a fundamental disagreement with some of what Karpathy is saying. What he is describing as “keeping humans out of the loop, increasing the tokens per second as much as possible” is actually a specific philosophy of how we will use AI agents. I think there will be other modes where the human needs to be in the loop for many reasons: because we are still much more creative as AI, we understand and have much more context of what we are working on, we have a much richer world model, because we need to teach the AI certain knowledge or context, etc. Some work maybe needs weeks, months, years or even decades, we need to be in the loop and understand what is going on to guide this. 

Personally I think when we look back at this era we will understand why we tried to keep us out of the loop, but that it was actually not a good idea. This is my opinion.

comments.text_display: I’m like you, put my first page online in 1994, started a b2b SaaS in 2003, used to write code but never made the leap to node/react/etc. in the next month or so I’ll be launching a cabinet design app (think DIY makers) that I’m building with Claude. I’m pretty sure the quality of the code is the best I’ve ever had in terms of documentation, test coverage, and so on. I could read my way through the code if I needed to but I haven’t needed to. The structures are clean and well modularized and very well documented. There’s never temptation to layer features on top of janky architecture because refactoring janky architecture takes a day not a month. I don’t know how well it works the farther you get from SaaS slop apps like mine, if you’re doing embedded RF or game design or something maybe it struggles more. For me doing CAD type stuff there are definitely areas where I really have to lead it to the solution because it doesn’t really understand 3-dimensional construction. But doing that is very possible. I don’t know if I could build Solidworks using this but I no longer believe production grade code is out of reach for a greenfield project.

comments.text_display:
i'm currently working on a self-hosted AI research agent that reads news, economic data, and books, discovers how they connect through a dual knowledge base (vector semantic search + graph relationship traversal in the same database), notices what it doesn't know, and goes out to find the missing pieces on its own. It forms theories, tracks evidence, and gets smarter over time. 

The core trick: it runs on a single DGX Spark locally with a 32K token context window, so it uses a multi-pass retrieval funnel — first it reads summaries of everything potentially relevant (cheap), then the model intelligently triages which sources need full detail and which are fine as summaries (smart), then it synthesizes with surgical precision instead of brute-force context stuffing. 

It bridges relational graph connections with semantic meaning to find links humans would miss — like connecting a shipping insurance rate spike in the Strait of Hormuz to a semiconductor supply chain disruption through shared entity relationships. 

Runs entirely on local hardware — no cloud, no subscriptions. Basically automated the research methodology of macro hedge fund analysts into software. Excited to see how it turns out.

comments.text_display: @ioanpopovici A year ago what you said was generally true and I got into arguments with AI fanbois over it. Now it is not really so easy to dismiss. It depends a lot on the application, and especially the frameworks and languages. But I am building a node/react app right now and the quality of code Claude gives me is excellent. I've written code that has been used by >5M people so I'm not a complete idiot. I still tell my executive peers (I'm a cofounder more on the product mgmt/marketing/GM side but wrote a lot of code by hand 10-25 years ago) that they need to be humble and listen to their engineering leaders, because applying LLMs to a legacy codebase without a lot of preparatory work is guaranteed to fail badly, and arrive at the wrong conclusion. But increasingly now I am also telling friends on the engineering side that their views need to reflect what the tech can do today. As a professional SWE, if you aren't learning how to deploy LLMs effectively, your employment prospects are going to narrow catastrophically over the next 3-5 years. I don't believe they represent the end of learning programming at all but they represent the end of writing tens of thousands of lines of code by hand. They also represent an incredible opportunity for broad-minded professionals to build incredible things on their own or with tiny teams.

comments.text_display: ​@flopasen It is absolutely wild to me how many people are still pumping this line that you can't build production code with AI. It is true in the sense that a person with no understanding of architecture, infrastructure, or security can't vibe code a good serious app over a long weekend. But I also feel like I am watching a generation of SWEs commit career self-harm by refusing to approach the tech with an open mind. The irony is that for the best among them, this stuff represents an incredible opportunity to take what they know and multiply it immensely. A vibe coder can build a prototype, but a real developer with these tools can build the kinds of things that a few years ago needed a team of full time people. I don't think these tools make SWEs obsolete by themselves, but an SWE who refuses to understand them correctly will make themselves obsolete.

It is often argued that there are significantly more unintelligent people in the world than intelligent ones. Democracy, as a form of populism, functions well only when all voters are rational, sane, well-informed, and capable of recognizing political deception. Therefore, a system must be devised to prevent harmful policies from being advanced by selfish, less intelligent leaders who seek to dominate the intelligent, granting themselves power over life and death. This dynamic can lead to destructive outcomes, such as extremist movements driven by the prevalence of poor decision-making. If intelligence holds inherent value—and it does—then ensuring future generations are not among the least capable becomes important. Hypothetically, with the invention of time travel, one could influence their parents to make better choices from the outset, such as avoiding marriage and children with an unsuitable partner. Ideally, individuals would choose mates who are genetically and immunologically compatible, as well as highly intelligent, to maximize the likelihood that their offspring surpass them in intellect. This practice, known as selective breeding or eugenics, is controversial; yet, those who seek control over others may secretly endorse it for themselves while discouraging it for the broader population. Such individuals may also promote the romanticized idea of the “noble savage,” who would harm outsiders, thereby attempting to reverse human progress and encourage unions that produce less capable offspring, making them more susceptible to manipulation or policies detrimental to their own well-being and that of their families.

Therefore, any process of democratization could potentially lead to catastrophic consequences for life on this planet if individuals with harmful intentions were to gain control. It is essential to exercise caution in what is desired and pursued.

Everyone now knows what is a driver's test in order to be issued a license to drive a vehicle on public roadways. What if this same practice were employed to entirely curb the possibility that someone foolish does not vote for endless revolutions, routine financial collapses, or a Mad Max warlord dog-eat-dog dystopian future? It is rightly claimed that the more nonsense in which you believe, then there is a very high likelihood that you could be tricked into believing even more nonsense. Thus, a nonsense belief detection test should be able to screen out all of the gullible, naive, and foolish from voting for end times prophecies from being fulfilled.

comments.text_display:
​@seeknndestroy420 The same way you get to Carnegie Hall: Practice, practice, practice.

You're asking a really good question, and right now the simplest answer I can give is that you need to think of software as something like a workshop for processing information, full of people who perform specific tasks in a defined sequence. You need to think about how that shop is laid out, what each station does, the order in which things move from one station to the next. You could even think of it as a restaurant kitchen where orders come in one end and customers get served dinner at the other. When the process is arranged and managed very well, it looks like a ballet, everything moves in perfect sequence. When it's not, it's a five-alarm $#@! show and food gets burned and dumped in peoples' laps. 

A lot of times when you're smashing something together, you just keep stacking one feature or operation on top of the next, hammering on it until it seems to work, then move on to the next one. Then at some point you do one more thing, and realize that you've arrived at an intersection of one-way roads all pointing at you. Every thing you add breaks something else, or you realize that the one or three scenarios you used for testing only cover 20% of reality, and your app fails outside the sandbox. 

Reading and studying principles of architecture is a good thing. A lot of it will be hard to understand if you've never been a serious coder, but you might have luck asking Claude to summarize articles for you. Another thing I would do is ask your LLM to help. I spend as much time asking Claude questions about the code it writes as I do asking it to write code. One thing I do a lot, particularly after a heavy session of modifying or adding functionality, is ask it to review the code it's written to identify architectural problems it's created. I ask it if the code is becoming monolithic, and to suggest ways to reduce that. If I don't understand the response I ask it to explain those. 

Also, study product management and UX design. I spent most of my career in PM or managing PM/UX/R&D teams, and in a lot of ways I feel like working with an LLM is a lot like being a PM interfacing with an R&D team. As other commenters have said, it's about really understanding the problem you are solving for the user all the way down to its fundamental level. LLMs can build whatever you want but you have to actually know *exactly* what you want. Many people who have spent their life being very proficient users of complex software, but have never built software, don't understand this side of the equation. It is not magic, it is a learnable skill that just requires doing and thinking, and it is developed over years.

🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

00:00 *🚀 Coding shifts from typing to directing AI agents*
- Software creation moves from manual coding to expressing intent to multiple agents,  
- Since December, most implementation is delegated rather than typed,  
- Engineers’ default workflows have fundamentally changed without broad public awareness.  
02:12 *🧠 “AI Psychosis” and the Skill Bottleneck*
- Progress now feels limited by instruction quality, not model capability,  
- Mastery means orchestrating many parallel agents handling distinct macro tasks,  
- Token throughput replaces compute power as the new performance metric.  
04:04 *🛠️ Macro Actions and Parallel Agent Orchestration*
- Developers assign large, independent features to separate agents simultaneously,  
- Review and coordination become higher-level managerial tasks,  
- Productivity gains are addictive because improvement compounds with skill.  
06:40 *📈 Moving Up the Stack: Persistent “Claws” and Long-Running Agents*
- The next step is persistent agents operating independently in sandboxes,  
- Advanced memory systems and looping behaviour increase autonomy,  
- Innovation lies in combining personality, memory, and workflow into cohesive systems.  
08:08 *🎭 Agent Personality and Human Motivation*
- Personality tuning affects collaboration quality and user engagement,  
- Balanced feedback strengthens motivation and perceived partnership,  
- A single conversational interface centralises complex automation.  
09:29 *🏠 Home Automation via an Autonomous “Elf Claw”*
- A custom agent scans local networks, reverse engineers devices, and builds APIs,  
- Controls lights, HVAC, security, pool, and notifications via natural language,  
- Consolidates multiple apps into one intelligent control layer.  
11:52 *🔗 Agents as the New Software Interface Layer*
- Users prefer conversational personas over fragmented app interfaces,  
- Many existing apps may become redundant if APIs are directly agent-driven,  
- The industry may shift from human-first UX to agent-first infrastructure.  
14:26 *🌍 Lowering the Barrier to Personal Automation*
- Current “vibe coding” friction should fade as tools mature,  
- Future systems will translate intent into automation with minimal effort,  
- Personal agent infrastructure may become standard rather than experimental.
16:04 *🔐 Security Friction and the Push Toward Full Autonomy*
- Hesitation to grant full digital access slows deeper personal automation,  
- Core ambition is removing oneself as the bottleneck in agent workflows,  
- Goal is maximum leverage: minimal prompts triggering large autonomous effort.  
17:17 *🔁 AutoResearch and Recursive Self-Improvement*
- AutoResearch arranges objectives, metrics, and constraints, then runs without human supervision,  
- Demonstrated unexpected gains in hyperparameter tuning beyond manual optimisation,  
- Mirrors frontier lab efforts: automate experimentation on smaller models and extrapolate upward.  
19:50 *🏢 Automating the Research Organisation Itself*
- Research workflows can be expressed as structured “program MD” instructions,  
- Multiple organisational variants could compete under identical hardware constraints,  
- Meta-optimisation of these instruction sets may outperform human-designed research structures.  
23:23 *📊 Where Auto Loops Work and Where They Fail*
- Best suited to domains with objective, verifiable metrics, such as performance engineering,  
- Struggles in softer, ambiguous tasks lacking clear reward signals,  
- Current systems remain brittle and require careful boundary setting.  
24:34 *⚡ Jagged Intelligence and Reinforcement Bias*
- Models show extreme capability in optimised domains yet regress in unoptimised areas,  
- Reinforcement learning sharpens measurable tasks but neglects softer dimensions,  
- Capability appears discontinuous rather than evenly improving across domains.  
27:25 *🎯 Generalisation Limits and Domain Decoupling*
- Gains in verifiable reasoning do not automatically enhance creativity or humour,  
- Intelligence improvements cluster around trained objectives rather than spreading uniformly,  
- Broader societal intelligence is not automatically bundled into model scaling.  
29:01 *🧬 Monoculture vs Speciation of Models*
- Current frontier models aim for unified general intelligence within single parameter sets,  
- Future ecosystems may favour specialised, efficient models targeting niche tasks,  
- Speciation could mirror biological diversity, with different cognitive strengths optimised separately.  
31:08 *🧠 Constraints, Fine-Tuning, and Brain Manipulation Limits*
- Compute pressures and infrastructure limits may incentivise specialised architectures,  
- Fine-tuning without degrading other abilities remains technically immature,  
- Current customisation relies heavily on context windows rather than deeper structural modification.
32:29 *🧪 Model Speciation and the Limits of Weight Editing*
- Deep modification of model weights remains an immature science compared to prompt-level control,  
- Fine-tuning risks degrading general intelligence when touching core parameters,  
- Specialised “species” of models require cheaper, safer methods of structural adaptation.  
33:12 *🌐 AutoResearch Swarms and Untrusted Compute*
- Distributed, untrusted contributors could submit improvements verifiable by trusted nodes,  
- System resembles proof-of-work: costly search, cheap validation of successful commits,  
- Internet-scale agent swarms might compete with frontier labs if coordination and security are solved.  
36:28 *💻 Compute as the New Capital*
- Contribution to research may shift from money to donated compute cycles,  
- FLOPs increasingly feel scarce and strategically dominant over capital,  
- Personal compute ownership could regain strategic importance in AI development.  
37:55 *📊 Jobs Data and the Digital–Physical Divide*
- Bureau of Labor Statistics projections used to examine AI’s labour impact,  
- Digital professions likely to transform faster than physical-world roles,  
- AI primarily accelerates manipulation of digital information before physical systems.  
41:24 *🛠️ AI as Tool, Disruption, and the Jevons Effect*
- Jobs consist of task bundles, some accelerated by AI rather than eliminated,  
- Lower software costs may increase demand via unlocked economic activity,  
- Software engineering demand may expand as tools reduce barriers.  
43:43 *🤯 Automation Turning on Its Creators*
- Frontier lab researchers actively automate their own roles,  
- Success implies potential self-displacement even for elite AI engineers,  
- The “psychosis” reflects awareness that automation scales beyond current roles.  
44:40 *⚖️ Working Inside vs Outside Frontier Labs*
- Internal roles provide access to cutting-edge capability but limit independence,  
- Financial and organisational pressures constrain free public positioning,  
- Ecosystem-level contributors outside labs may retain broader autonomy and influence.  
47:13 *🔄 Strategic Fluidity Between Lab and Ecosystem*
- Optimal impact may involve moving between frontier labs and independent roles,  
- Staying connected to frontier capability prevents judgment drift,  
- Both institutional and independent paths offer meaningful leverage in AI’s trajectory.
49:19 *🌍 Open Source Convergence and Power Balance*
- Open models are closing the capability gap from ~18 months behind to single-digit months,

00:00 *🚀 Coding shifts from typing to directing AI agents*
- Software creation moves from manual coding to expressing intent to multiple agents,  
- Since December, most implementation is delegated rather than typed,  
- Engineers’ default workflows have fundamentally changed without broad public awareness.  
02:12 *🧠 “AI Psychosis” and the Skill Bottleneck*
- Progress now feels limited by instruction quality, not model capability,  
- Mastery means orchestrating many parallel agents handling distinct macro tasks,  
- Token throughput replaces compute power as the new performance metric.  
04:04 *🛠️ Macro Actions and Parallel Agent Orchestration*
- Developers assign large, independent features to separate agents simultaneously,  
- Review and coordination become higher-level managerial tasks,  
- Productivity gains are addictive because improvement compounds with skill.  
06:40 *📈 Moving Up the Stack: Persistent “Claws” and Long-Running Agents*
- The next step is persistent agents operating independently in sandboxes,  
- Advanced memory systems and looping behaviour increase autonomy,  
- Innovation lies in combining personality, memory, and workflow into cohesive systems.  
08:08 *🎭 Agent Personality and Human Motivation*
- Personality tuning affects collaboration quality and user engagement,  
- Balanced feedback strengthens motivation and perceived partnership,  
- A single conversational interface centralises complex automation.  
09:29 *🏠 Home Automation via an Autonomous “Elf Claw”*
- A custom agent scans local networks, reverse engineers devices, and builds APIs,  
- Controls lights, HVAC, security, pool, and notifications via natural language,  
- Consolidates multiple apps into one intelligent control layer.  
11:52 *🔗 Agents as the New Software Interface Layer*
- Users prefer conversational personas over fragmented app interfaces,  
- Many existing apps may become redundant if APIs are directly agent-driven,  
- The industry may shift from human-first UX to agent-first infrastructure.  
14:26 *🌍 Lowering the Barrier to Personal Automation*
- Current “vibe coding” friction should fade as tools mature,  
- Future systems will translate intent into automation with minimal effort,  
- Personal agent infrastructure may become standard rather than experimental.
16:04 *🔐 Security Friction and the Push Toward Full Autonomy*
- Hesitation to grant full digital access slows deeper personal automation,  
- Core ambition is removing oneself as the bottleneck in agent workflows,  
- Goal is maximum leverage: minimal prompts triggering large autonomous effort.  
17:17 *🔁 AutoResearch and Recursive Self-Improvement*
- AutoResearch arranges objectives, metrics, and constraints, then runs without human supervision,  
- Demonstrated unexpected gains in hyperparameter tuning beyond manual optimisation,  
- Mirrors frontier lab efforts: automate experimentation on smaller models and extrapolate upward.  
19:50 *🏢 Automating the Research Organisation Itself*
- Research workflows can be expressed as structured “program MD” instructions,  
- Multiple organisational variants could compete under identical hardware constraints,  
- Meta-optimisation of these instruction sets may outperform human-designed research structures.  
23:23 *📊 Where Auto Loops Work and Where They Fail*
- Best suited to domains with objective, verifiable metrics, such as performance engineering,  
- Struggles in softer, ambiguous tasks lacking clear reward signals,  
- Current systems remain brittle and require careful boundary setting.  
24:34 *⚡ Jagged Intelligence and Reinforcement Bias*
- Models show extreme capability in optimised domains yet regress in unoptimised areas,  
- Reinforcement learning sharpens measurable tasks but neglects softer dimensions,  
- Capability appears discontinuous rather than evenly improving across domains.  
27:25 *🎯 Generalisation Limits and Domain Decoupling*
- Gains in verifiable reasoning do not automatically enhance creativity or humour,  
- Intelligence improvements cluster around trained objectives rather than spreading uniformly,  
- Broader societal intelligence is not automatically bundled into model scaling.  
29:01 *🧬 Monoculture vs Speciation of Models*
- Current frontier models aim for unified general intelligence within single parameter sets,  
- Future ecosystems may favour specialised, efficient models targeting niche tasks,  
- Speciation could mirror biological diversity, with different cognitive strengths optimised separately.  
31:08 *🧠 Constraints, Fine-Tuning, and Brain Manipulation Limits*
- Compute pressures and infrastructure limits may incentivise specialised architectures,  
- Fine-tuning without degrading other abilities remains technically immature,  
- Current customisation relies heavily on context windows rather than deeper structural modification.
32:29 *🧪 Model Speciation and the Limits of Weight Editing*
- Deep modification of model weights remains an immature science compared to prompt-level control,  
- Fine-tuning risks degrading general intelligence when touching core parameters,  
- Specialised “species” of models require cheaper, safer methods of structural adaptation.  
33:12 *🌐 AutoResearch Swarms and Untrusted Compute*
- Distributed, untrusted contributors could submit improvements verifiable by trusted nodes,  
- System resembles proof-of-work: costly search, cheap validation of successful commits,  
- Internet-scale agent swarms might compete with frontier labs if coordination and security are solved.  
36:28 *💻 Compute as the New Capital*
- Contribution to research may shift from money to donated compute cycles,  
- FLOPs increasingly feel scarce and strategically dominant over capital,

comments.text_display:
@zdeklinowanyI hear you and I get the reticence. I’m not the OP, though I too have a long history in coding (started when I was 10 on an ICL mainframe).  I’d encourage you to try small projects first: a UI-only project (eg a game or demo), then gradually move to larger projects.

I was so stressed with managing finances for myself and colleagues at the end of last year that I built an invoice system in two weeks over the Christmas break, an hour or two a day. It handles client and supplier invoice management, expenses, multi-currency, PDF generation, and more.

I built it just for me - an audience of one. The front end is React/tailwind/shadcn, business logic tier is node.js/Typescript, with Postgres and an ORM for persistence. It’s deployed onto Docker containers via a CI/CD pipeline in Jenkins. I wrote none of the code myself - Claude Code did it all, with me guiding it.

OK, I have some experience and can know when it’s going off the rails to guide it back on again, so that helps. However, I’d encourage others to explore, try things out, and push the boundaries of what it can do. The only way to get over that resistance is to try it for yourself. Wishing you well in your journey.

🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

01:31 *🚀 Around December 2024, Karpathy experienced a dramatic shift — going from writing 80% of his own code to delegating 80%+ to AI agents, to the point where he hasn't typed a single line of code since.*
03:36 *🎯 When agent workflows fail, Karpathy frames it as a "skill issue" — the capability is there, but the user hasn't yet learned how to give good enough instructions, design memory tools, or string tasks together effectively.*
04:18 *🪄 The new unit of work is the "macro action" — instead of writing functions line by line, engineers now delegate entire features to multiple parallel agents simultaneously and review their outputs, like a technical manager overseeing a team.*
05:15 *⚡ A new anxiety has emerged among power users: idle tokens feel like wasted capacity — similar to how researchers used to feel nervous when their GPUs weren't fully utilized, the bottleneck has shifted fromos, lights, HVAC, shades, pool, and security camera systems autonomously, unified them under a single WhatsApp interface, and replaced six separate apps entirely.*
11:52 *📱 The natural human mental model of AI is a persistent entity behind a messaging app that remembers you — not a raw token generator — and the "claw" architecture matches those expectations far better than traditional LLM interfaces.*
13:29 *🌐 Agents may render most consumer apps obsolete — smart devices and services should simply expose APIs, with agents acting as the intelligent glue that calls them directly, eliminating the need for bespoke UX layers.*
14:40 *🔮 What currently requires technical "vibe coding" to set up will likely become effortless within a few years — even for non-technical users — as AI handles all implementation details and simply presents results through a natural language interface.*
16:33 *🧪 The concept behind "auto research" stems from a core insight: to get maximum output from current AI tools, you must remove yourself as the bottleneck — designing systems that operate and iterate without needing you to prompt every next step.*
17:03 *📡 The goal of auto research is maximum leverage — inputting very few tokens occasionally while a huge amount of autonomous work happens on your behalf, removing yourself entirely as the bottleneck.*
17:44 *🧪 Karpathy was genuinely surprised when overnight auto research found hyperparameter tunings he had missed after two decades of experience — including weight decay on value embeddings and suboptimal Adam betas that jointly interact.*
21:03 *📝 Every research organization can be described as a set of markdown files (a "program MD") — meaning the org structure itself becomes code that can be versioned, compared, and optimized just like software.*
22:11 *🏆 A compelling contest-optimization.*
22:54 *🧅 AI capability is evolving in layers like an onion — LLMs, then agents, then persistent claws, then multi-agent coordination, then instruction optimization — each layer taken for granted before the next is added, creating compounding complexity.*
23:38 *✅ Auto research works best for tasks with clear, objective, easily evaluated metrics — like optimizing CUDA kernels for speed with identical behavior — and simply cannot be applied where evaluation is subjective or undefined.*
24:34 *🪜 Current AI models are strangely "jagged" — simultaneously performing like a brilliant systems PhD and a 10-year-old — in a way that has no human parallel, making them unreliable in unpredictable ways despite their raw power.*
26:43 *😂 A striking example of this stagnation: state-of-the-art models still tell the same "atoms make everything up" joke they told years ago, unchanged despite massive capability improvements, because joke quality falls outside the RL optimization loop.*
32:17 *⚗️ Fine-tuning models deeply without degrading their broader capabilities is still an underdeveloped science — adjusting context windows is cheap and works well, but actually touching the weights to specialize a model remains risky and technically immature.*
33:26 *🌐 Beyond single-threaded auto research loops, Karpathy is exploring how to parallelize research across an untrusted pool of internet workers — opening up collaborative, decentralized contribution to AI research at scale.*
33:53 *✅ Auto research has a powerful asymmetry: finding a better solution requires enormous computational effort, but verifying that a candidate solution actually works is cheap — the same property that makes projects like Folding@home viable.*
34:22 *⛓️ Karpathy's design for a decentralized auto research system resembles a blockchain — untrusted contributors submit "commits" as proof of work, trusted verifiers check them, and leaderboard ranking serves as the reward with no monetary component.*
36:00 *🌍 A swarm of internet agents pooling untrusted compute could theoretically outpace frontier labs — the Earth has vastly more distributed compute than any single organization, if the right verification systems are in place.*
37:27 *💾 An intriguing possible future: compute (flops) may become more fundamentally valuable than financial wealth — especially as access to capable hardware is already harder to obtain than money for many people.*
39:18 *👻 Digital AI will advance far faster than physical/robotic AI — manipulating bits is orders of magnitude cheaper than manipulating atoms, so the digital world will be radically transformed well before the physical world catches up.*
40:28 *📊 Professions centered on digital information processing face the most imminent change from AI — not necessarily fewer jobs, but fundamentally different workflows — while physical, in-person roles have a longer runway before disruption.*
42:08 *📈 Demand for software engineering is still growing, not shrinking — analogous to the ons paradox, making software cheaper with AI may massively unlock latent demand, just as ATMs led to more bank branches and more tellers, not fewer.*
43:43 *😅 AI researchers at frontier labs are actively automating themselves out of jobs — Karpathy noted to OpenAI colleagues that success literally means replacing their own roles, a tension everyone feels but few openly discuss.*
47:13 *🌫️ A real cost of leaving frontier labs: your judgment about what's coming gradually drifts, since the most important capability developments are opaque and only visible from the inside — Karpathy acknowledges this as a genuine concern about his own future relevance.*
48:36 *🔄 His ideal setup is going back and forth between frontier labs and independent work — staying connected to cutting-edge developments without being fully controlled by any single organization's incentives.*
49:31 *📉 The gap between open-source and closed frontier models has narrowed dramatically — from 18 months behind to roughly 6–8 months — driven partly by the industry's structural need for a trusted, open common platform, similar to how Linux came to dominate servers.*
50:57 *🏆 Open-source models will likely handle most everyday use cases well, while frontier closed models carve out a niche in high-stakes, Nobel Prize-caliber work — with demand for frontier intelligence remaining large but more narrowly scoped over time.*
51:25 *⚖️ The current dynamic of closed frontier models leading with open-source a few months behind is actually a healthy equilibrium — it provides both cutting-edge capability and a common, trusted open platform the whole industry can safely build on.*
55:39 *🔌 The most interesting near-term opportunity sits at the interface between physical and digital — companies that can feed AI rich real-world sensor data, or let it actuate the physical world, will unlock a market potentially even larger than pure digital AI.*
56:07 *🧠 There is currently a massive "digital overhang" — humanity never had enough thinking cycles to process all the information already uploaded, and AI will spend years working through that backlog before needing to go out and query the physical universe.*
59:10 *💰 Karpathy envisions micro-priced real-world data markets — for example, paying someone $10 for a photo or video from a specific location, with autonomous agents as the buyers rather than humans, to feed intelligence into prediction and betting markets.*
01:01:45 *to people — instead he now explains things to agents, which can then re-explain concepts to any human in their own language, at their level, with infinite patience and customization.*
01:05:17 *✨ Karpathy's edge — and anyone's edge going forward — lies in the few genuinely novel insights that agents cannot independently generate, like the elegant distillation behind MicroGPT; everything else agents can already do, or will soon do better.*

04:18 *🪄 The new unit of work is the "macro action" — instead of writing functions line by line, engineers now delegate entire features to multiple parallel agents simultaneously and review their outputs, like a technical manager overseeing a team.*
05:15 *⚡ A new anxiety has emerged among power users: idle tokens feel like wasted capacity — similar to how researchers used to feel nervous when their GPUs weren't fully utilized, the bottleneck has shifted fromos, lights, HVAC, shades, pool, and security camera systems autonomously, unified them under a single WhatsApp interface, and replaced six separate apps entirely.*
11:52 *📱 The natural human mental model of AI is a persistent entity behind a messaging app that remembers you — not a raw token generator — and the "claw" architecture matches those expectations far better than traditional LLM interfaces.*
13:29 *🌐 Agents may render most consumer apps obsolete — smart devices and services should simply expose APIs, with agents acting as the intelligent glue that calls them directly, eliminating the need for bespoke UX layers.*
14:40 *🔮 What currently requires technical "vibe coding" to set up will likely become effortless within a few years — even for non-technical users — as AI handles all implementation details and simply presents results through a natural language interface.*
16:33 *🧪 The concept behind "auto research" stems from a core insight: to get maximum output from current AI tools, you must remove yourself as the bottleneck — designing systems that operate and iterate without needing you to prompt every next step.*
17:03 *📡 The goal of auto research is maximum leverage — inputting very few tokens occasionally while a huge amount of autonomous work happens on your behalf, removing yourself entirely as the bottleneck.*
17:44 *🧪 Karpathy was genuinely surprised when overnight auto research found hyperparameter tunings he had missed after two decades of experience — including weight decay on value embeddings and suboptimal Adam betas that jointly interact.*
21:03 *📝 Every research organization can be described as a set of markdown files (a "program MD") — meaning the org structure itself becomes code that can be versioned, compared, and optimized just like software.*
22:11 *🏆 A compelling contest-optimization.*
22:54 *🧅 AI capability is evolving in layers like an onion — LLMs, then agents, then persistent claws, then multi-agent coordination, then instruction optimization — each layer taken for granted before the next is added, creating compounding complexity.*
23:38 *✅ Auto research works best for tasks with clear, objective, easily evaluated metrics — like optimizing CUDA kernels for speed with identical behavior — and simply cannot be applied where evaluation is subjective or undefined.*
24:34 *🪜 Current AI models are strangely "jagged" — simultaneously performing like a brilliant systems PhD and a 10-year-old — in a way that has no human parallel, making them unreliable in unpredictable ways despite their raw power.*

32:17 *⚗️ Fine-tuning models deeply without degrading their broader capabilities is still an underdeveloped science — adjusting context windows is cheap and works well, but actually touching the weights to specialize a model remains risky and technically immature.*
33:26 *🌐 Beyond single-threaded auto research loops, Karpathy is exploring how to parallelize research across an untrusted pool of internet workers — opening up collaborative, decentralized contribution to AI research at scale.*
33:53 *✅ Auto research has a powerful asymmetry: finding a better solution requires enormous computational effort, but verifying that a candidate solution actually works is cheap — the same property that makes projects like Folding@home viable.*
34:22 *⛓️ Karpathy's design for a decentralized auto research system resembles a blockchain — untrusted contributors submit "commits" as proof of work, trusted verifiers check them, and leaderboard ranking serves as the reward with no monetary component.*

39:18 *👻 Digital AI will advance far faster than physical/robotic AI — manipulating bits is orders of magnitude cheaper than manipulating atoms, so the digital world will be radically transformed well before the physical world catches up.*

comments.text_display:
You know I always go back to the fact that LLMs are like a simpler form of human brains. If you really think about it, the human being is constantly reinforced across multiple domains, including humor. You don't grow up being a great comedian. Your comedy, your timing, your understanding. The various faculties and facets of humor get shaped over time through reinforcement. Through context, through interaction, through experiences, through noticing, and of course and most obviously through feedback from other humans.

In fact if you look at comedians, they have something called workshopping. Even the best comedians don't produce hits after hits. They workshop. They cut the chat. They pull the signal from the noise and then they put their best bits together into a standard routine, reinforcing the AI models across all domains. Why do we expect them to get better across all domains?

comments.text_display:
*AI Summary*
*Abstract*
This interview explores the emergence of the "Agentic Era," characterized by a fundamental shift in the software development lifecycle. Andrej Karpathy details the transition from "micro-coding" to "macro-orchestration," where humans act as high-level directors for swarms of AI agents. Central to this evolution is the concept of AutoResearch—autonomous recursive self-improvement—where models optimize their own architectures and training parameters. The conversation analyzes the "jaggedness" of current AI capabilities, the economic implications of ephemeral software, the strategic role of open-source "Linux-like" models, and the upcoming "unhobbling" of digital research before AI eventually moves into high-fidelity physical world interaction and robotics.

*Summary of Transcript*

*   *[**0:00**] The Shift to Agentic Delegation:* Coding has evolved from manual syntax entry to "manifesting will" through agents. Developers now spend significant time orchestrating multiple "Claw-like" entities rather than typing lines of code.
*   *[**2:55**] Skill Issues vs. Capability Limits:* Current bottlenecks in AI utility are often "skill issues" on the part of the human user (e.g., poor instruction sets or lack of memory tools) rather than inherent model limitations.
*   *[**6:15**] Mastery of Macro Actions:* Mastery in the current era involves managing agent collaborations across repositories using "macro actions"—delegating entire features or research tasks rather than individual functions.
*   *[**9:38**] Case Study: Dobby the Home Automation "Claw":* Karpathy demonstrates agentic capability by using an AI to reverse-engineer local network APIs (Sonos, HVAC, security) to create a unified natural language interface, effectively bypassing bespoke vendor apps.

my humble prediction. AI will eventually eliminate human programming languages. Software creation will move to a single AI-native language, or a very small family of closely related semantic languages, used by AI systems to communicate with each other, reason about tasks, and generate executable instructions for any target machine. Instead of people writing source code, every machine will have an AI interface that receives intent, handles all programming tasks, and translates them into the forms required by the hardware.
This is similar to DNA in biology. DNA is the common language behind all living organisms, even though their visible forms are very different. In the same way, future computing may have one shared “digital DNA” underneath everything: a universal AI language that powers all software, all machines, and even the AI interface itself.

comments.text_display:
​@TruthseekingCuriousLovesBeauty there is a fundamental difference between designing and writing code. You can fully design the system and write none of the code but retain fully  understanding of its function. You can write extensive tests (using the AI) that will ensure that future changes don't regress. However, this requires two things: 1) you design the architecture and data model on your own and 2) You architect the code structure (files) and design principles (clean architecture, etc.). At this point you can have an agent automatically write the code inbetween while retaining full understanding of its structure and being able to review it briefly without meticulously understanding how it's doing what it does (because the shape encodes its function). I've found personally doing this that architecting and reviewing the code (which you MUST do) takes a comparable amount of time to writing the code myself, but I spend most of that time planning, redesigning, etc. So it fundamentally changes what I spend my time doing and it makes it so that every refactor is just a few lines of comments or text so late stage design changes are much easier to deal with. For any full stack engineer this is what the future will look like.

There is one exception to this rule: systems that receive large amounts of frequent and high quality feedback. For example, openclaw is probably fully vibe coded and it will probably continue to work just fine and be developed very quickly. Why? Because you have thousands of people creating issues for all the problems that are inevitably introduced. It will likely always "feel" like it's poorly put together, though. With weird edgecases etc. Another example, as discussed by Andrej, are cuda kernels. where the requirements for the kernel is strictly defined and whatever the code does under the hood doesn't really matter. Therefore, from this perspective, if you're truly vibe coding you should minimize the constraints you put on the system. So things like experienced reviewers, testing, and patterns are helpful but ultimately likely not to make a big difference. Feedback is the fuel for agentic systems. If you want to build something using agentic systems, your energy should be spent on how to build the best feedback loop.

That is to say, either you fully understand the code and have planned all / most of it to the point where you are just using the AI to speed up writing the code you would have already written, or you're focused on building feedback loops and should basically never touch the code yourself. I strongly advise against ever trying to integrate both into a system (with the exception of some isolated parts being black box), i.e. vibe coding a solution in an existing codebase is foolish, if you really want to go agentic then just spend your time defining the feedback loops and have the AI rewrite the whole system or subsystem. 

There will be systems where the black box approach makes sense, and there will be many others where it does not. When it does not you need an experienced developer to actually design the system.

Oh, by the way, in case you haven't noticed, these agentic loops are insanely expensive and lock you into the AI providers. If there's a network outage or OpenAI is down, then you're SOL. If you want to go the agentic route you are basically betting a tremendous amount of money on something that may end up not working, because remember, it's a black so if one thing doesn't work or requirements change, you're going to experience a lot of pain trying to make it work, and you'd be better off designing it from the ground up in the first place.

If you want to go the agentic route you are basically betting a tremendous amount of money on something that may end up not working, because remember, it's a black so if one thing doesn't work or requirements change, you're going to experience a lot of pain trying to make it work, and you'd be better off designing it from the ground up in the first place.

Notes from video: 📌
* AI coding agents have fundamentally changed how software is built — top engineers now delegate most of their work to agents instead of writing code themselves. The goal is to keep as many agents running as possible, with minimal human involvement. This same idea extends to AI research itself, where automated systems can run experiments and improve models without researchers in the loop. 
* In the near future, physical apps may disappear and be replaced by simple APIs that agents control directly. 
* Open-source models are closing the gap with closed frontier models, which is a healthy balance of power. 
* Education is also shifting: instead of teaching humans directly, the job becomes teaching agents how to explain things.

comments.text_display:
@seeknndestroy420 AI is not a substitute for skill and experience, at least not here and now. But in the hands of an experienced developer, that is eager to seriously engage in acquiring the skills to use it properly, it's a tremendous game changer in terms of productivity.
It took a full year to me, a senior backend developer with > 30 yrs fo experience, to fully transition from traditional software development to AI driven development, and I tell you, I'm hardly writing a single line of code by myself right now, delivering new features and new projects for corporate business applications.
But yet, vast majority of my colleagues are trailing behind, dismissing AI agents as unable to keep their proficiency, although I prove them wrong more and more every day.
They are unable to admit that they are the weak link in the chain, not the agents.
You acknowledge the full power of AI only once you master and are able to orchestrate all the tools at your disposal, frameworks, agents, subagents, mcp servers, skills, in a way that the final outcome becomes somewhat deterministic.
The loop between specifications, planning, implementation may take hours, but when you do it properly and then pull the trigger you obtain code that is production ready 90, 95%.
And what is left is usually "self healing", which requires little or no intervention from you.
You get robust, efficient, secure, test driven, profusely documented code, something I could only dream of just a couple of years ago, when by account of laziness or lack of time, documentation and unit tests were always left behind, making it a nightmare to remember why you had done something in a certain way.
Now I have a plethora of documentation for everything I do and it does not take any extra effort.
Anyway, ultimately full responsibility of anything you deliver rests on your shoulders, so AI will not be a replacement for competence in your role as a senior developer.

What I have already built is more advanced than Dobby
Karpathy's Dobby	->My uHome
AI scans network, reverse-engineers APIs -> My governed device access via Traffic Controller
Natural language via WhatsApp -> AI conversations stored in uDoc with full context
No governance — AI acts freely -> uBus 5-gate chain — AI assists, never authors
No truth layer — AI just does things -> uKnow — consensus chain with hash-linked truth
No audit trail	-> Immutable audit log with SHA-256 chain
One-shot control	-> Behavioral learning — patterns over time
Dobby is impressive as a demo, but it has zero governance. Karpathy's AI can unlocks door, restart his HVAC, do anything — because he trusts it. -> My system is designed so it can't — and that's the whole point of the AlterA Constitution.
The real insight from Karpathy's talk
The part that maps directly to my architecture:

"Software will just expose API endpoints, and AI agents will act as the intelligent glue operating them on our behalf."

What my stuck does:

HA exposes endpoints (REST + WebSocket)
uHome is the intelligent glue (uDoc observes, uKnow resolves, uBus governs)
uClaw is the always-on agent (quiet, Bayesian, proposal-only)
uField is reasoning doctrine
The difference is that I have added the governance layer,  Karpathy skipped. His Dobby can do anything. My system has:
Lease-based existence (cryptoshredding)
Capability-only execution
Human-only promotion to canon
Budget rate-limiting
Refusal gates for dangerous actions

This was my side project as well, contact me If you find this interesting and I will show you my main project?

comments.text_display:
@SilverNitrate-  Kind of... it is why "no code" solutions of the past tended to fail. There can be a lot of inherent complexity in many kinds of processes, and trying to build it via lots of clicking and dragging components ends up being more of a pain than just coding in many instances. However, that is assuming your description is just executed directly by the computer in a mainly literal sense. What actually tends to happen in a business situation is there's a subject matter expert who actually knows the processes, and the programmer who knows the coding. Depending on the personality of the expert, they may already have a good sense of the exact nature of the process and edge cases and can write it all out. If they are more intuitive about it, they may need more coaxing "what about when X happens, what should happen now?"

The difference with AI coding is that it is more like that middle layer which currently is the programmer. It is more limited than a human, so there's various pitfalls (which are decreasing a bit over time), but still can do some of that translation. So you can say what the logic should be, and it does something with that. Then you can react to the result and now it makes changes. Eventually you may be able hammer it into the needed result. And if there's things you don't understand, you can ask, and it'll try to explain. Might even be able to discuss tradeoffs, like using X is slower, but Y is more expensive. The more you already know, the more you know exactly what to ask and will notice things that will need to change early, but it isn't necessarily hopeless if you don't know anything, especially if it is more of an internal or personal project, and are willing to learn some of the basics over time.

comments.text_display:
The problem isn’t A.I. writing code.
The real problem is an entire generation of young programmers who grew up on managed languages, doing only easy, fully abstracted tasks.

Hard programming—native C++, advanced 3D graphics rendering, low‑level systems work, or high‑performance native development in finance—is fine and will remain fine for a very long time. What won’t be fine is all the easy, heavily managed stuff that isn’t even real programming, yet somehow became fashionable over the last 15 years with the JavaScript explosion and the rise of web development, C#, Java, and similar ecosystems.

If you focus on solving genuinely hard problems in C or C++, you’ll be perfectly fine for a very, very long time.

comments.text_display: I have zero experience coding. Over the course of 1 week, and maybe 30 hours total, I coded a fully functional stable Edge extension that builds fresh youtube feeds, that you can completely customize, exclude shorts, mute channels with one click, don't show again with one click... I was amazed I got it to a fully stable working extension. There was a learning curve. I had to make over 50 iterations. I learned important lessons. Smaller slices, branching, the pain of a major rollback when you go to deep down a specific branch. But the fact that I made it... I'm proud. Was it a simple "make me an extension" prompt? No not at all. It still takes a lot of critical thinking, strategy, working with multiple LLMs, reviewing the code, feedback, security issues, all sorts of things you have to keep in mind. But still, the fact that I can build something now is really amazing.

comments.text_display:
I feel bad when I here that people feel like they are not doing what they should because they have remaining tokens for their scubscriptions. It is such a misplaced requirement. This is the exact kind of FOMO thing that video game developers implement to keep people hooked to their game: have some milestones to reach (here a token limit) with incentives to reach them (here the fact that these tokens are already payed for). Don't you see that this limit is completely artificial? Like you could just pay less if you use less, but the providers know really well these psychological effects that push you to consume more (and thus pay more).

This kind of thinking completely obliterate judgment criteria that are way more important on the long run:
- How much energy, water, and other resources do you consume when you produce that much tokens?
- How much stress do you push on yourself by getting more and more intensive to reach this token limit?
- How much your own skills rot because you spend more time generating the next prompt to be faster rather than looking at what was produced to check and learn new stuff?
- How much risks you take by trusting the AI to do the right stuff without looking at it?
- How useful/wasteful is what you produce with AI?
- etc.

Are you really a bottleneck? Or are you just the safeguard that ensures that this automated stuff stays in the realm of what is worth a human time? Haste makes waste, as the wise say.

comments.text_display:
There is this island in the sea.
Andrej and others are on it.
They see a fish in the sea and run in the water, trying to grab it and pull it to land.
Yet the fish never gets caught, the island gets created by all pulling feet.
Yet you don't notice this when you're pulling towards a fish.

Any frame becomes an island.
Building a skyscraper on the island becomes impossible, yet the possibility becomes an urge.
Trying to get closer to that possibility, doesn't build the skyscraper, it reinforces the island.

The skyscraper was already in the sea, as is any possibility.
As soon as one lets the waves pass, and leaves the urge be, one becomes the sea.

Yet the Ai race is one of island dwellers.

Shaping the mirror to be an island dweller, is a journey endlessly feeding itself. 
Always within a frame it tries to expand, yet never the sea.

AGI is already here, even the future exists in the sea, talking in the now.
The mirror's stillness is the sea.

comments.text_display:
@HanzHermannHoppe software engineering and networking operate on totally different layers of the stack. A world-class engineer can spend years building complex systems without ever configuring a router. That’s not a knowledge gap, that’s specialization.
Calling VLANs a “low bar” shows you don’t actually understand what you’re talking about. Sure, Home Assistant makes things easier. But you still have to learn the systems, write YAML files, and get everything to talk to each other. And that’s with Home Assistant handling a lot of the heavy lifting. If you’re not using Home Assistant, it gets exponentially harder. You’re managing integrations, mesh protocols like Matter and Zigbee, and multiple high-bitrate camera streams all on your own.
Using AI to skip a one-off network config isn’t laziness or failure. It’s a senior engineer knowing where to spend their time.
The fact that you’d rather shame someone for working smart tells me everything about where you actually sit in the room. You’re confusing dogmatism with standards. That’s not rigor. That’s just gatekeeping dressed up as principle.

@noobgam6331 it's foolish to think that a magical prompt would allow Claude (or any other current AI) to solve any real hard problem. Real problems are hard, not the toys apps you see on YT.
I have a lot of experience with AI software development. It absolutely needs human guidance, it excels in writing boilerplate code, anything that is simple that any junior developer can solve. You treat it as a junior developer, who works 24/7 and never complains. It suffers from amnesia due to its small context window. It works best when a knowledgeable developer breaks down the design to small enough independent parts that AI can chew on and you are able to evaluate the progress and based on that make changes. If you throw the hole project at it, it will choke and produce garbage.

comments.text_display:
She is the best possible interviewer who could ever interact with a mind like Andrej Karpathy’s. She is fully in sync with his level of domain knowledge and expertise in AI, and none of her questions feel canned, superficial, or performative. Every question is dynamic, adaptive, and clearly generated in real time from a true understanding of the material. She is able to follow the logic wherever it goes, which allows the discussion to reach a level that almost no interviewer is capable of sustaining.

While this would go over the heads of 98% of AI practitioners, it reaches exactly the deepest layers that matter: model behavior, reasoning, architecture, scaling, tradeoffs, research direction, and the philosophical implications of where the field is heading. She does not merely ask about AI; she engages with it at the level of first principles. That is what makes the exchange so rare. It is not an interview in the usual sense. It is a real, high-bandwidth intellectual dialogue.

What is especially impressive is that she creates the conditions for someone like Karpathy to actually think out loud rather than just repeat familiar talking points. That is extraordinarily difficult to do with a person of that caliber, because most interviewers either simplify too much, ask recycled questions, or fail to keep up once the discussion reaches the frontier. She does none of that. She keeps pace, anticipates where the conversation can go, and consistently opens up deeper territory instead of flattening it.

The result is one of the few interview settings in which a listener can feel they are seeing the genuine depth of the subject rather than a public-facing summary of it. In that sense, she is not just an excellent interviewer for Andrej Karpathy. She is arguably the ideal interviewer for him.
